# MAIN ARTICLE # ORIGINAL PAPER # Visual determination of hydrogen peroxide and glucose by exploiting the peroxidase-like activity of magnetic nanoparticles functionalized with a poly(ethylene glycol) derivative Ho Yun Shin1 & Bong-Geun Kim2 & Seongyeon Cho1 & Jongwon Lee2 & Hyon Bin Na2 & Moon Il Kim1 Received: 21November2016 /Accepted: 18March2017 # Springer-Verlag Wien2017 Abstract A colorimetric assay is described for the determination of H2O2and glucose based on the peroxidasemimicking activity of Fe3O4magnetic nanoparticles functionalized with a poly(ethylene glycol) derivative (PEG-MNPs). PEG ligands conjugated to a phosphine oxide and a phospholipid were synthesized and deposited on the surface of magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs). This leads to enhanced peroxidase-like activity of the MNPs by providing more monodispersed MNPs to be present in solution and by reducing self-aggregation. The PEG-MNPs were then used as enzyme mimetics that catalyze the oxidation of2,2 ′ -azino-bis(3ethylbenzo-thiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) to form a green product having an absorption maximum at417nm which enables a visual detection of H2O2. These findings are exploited in a highly sensitive and selective glucose bioassay based on the formation of H2O2by glucose oxidase (GOx). The calibration plot covers the glucose concentration range from5 μ M to Electronic supplementary material The online version of this article (doi:10.1007/s00604-017-2198-z) contains supplementary material, which is available to authorized users. - Hyon Bin Na hyonbin@mju.ac.kr - Moon Il Kim moonil@gachon.ac.kr - 1Department of BioNano Technology, Gachon University, 1342Seongnamdae-ro, Sujeong-gu, Seongnam, Gyeonggi13120, Republic of Korea - 2Department of Chemical Engineering, Myongji University, 116Myongji-ro, Cheoin-gu, Yongin, Gyeonggi17058, Republic of Korea 1mM, and the limit of detection is as low as3 μ M. The diagnostic capability of the method is demonstrated by analyzing glucose in human blood serum. It is perceived that the PEG-MNPs presented here also may act as potent peroxidase mimetics in other methods for the determination of clinically important analytes. Keywords Colorimetric biosensor . Peroxidase mimics . Glucose detection . Monodispersed magnetic nanoparticles . Surface coating # Introduction Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) of the type Fe3O4have attracted significant attention as potent peroxidase mimetics [1]. Compared to natural peroxidase enzymes, they exhibited highly stable catalytic activity over a broad range of pH and temperature values and over a long incubation or storage period. They were also relatively amenable to a conjugation reaction with other biological receptor molecules, such as DNA, antibodies, or enzymes, and they can be produced at a significantly low cost with a mass yield. Since the pioneering study for the first investigation of the intrinsic peroxidase activity of Fe3O4MNPs, a significant amount of research has been devoted to demonstrating the possible applications of MNPs [2]. For example, the peroxidase activity of MNPs has been utilized to detect various molecules, such as H2O2, glucose and other carbohydrates, small molecules, DNA, proteins, and cells, by employing colorimetric substrates, such as2,2 ′ -azino-bis(3-ethylbenzo-thiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) or3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), or N , N -diethylp -pheny lenediamine sulfate (DPD) [3 -10]. Electrochemical detection strategies using MNPs, MNPs assembled on a carbon nanotube, or a conductive nanocomposite consisting of MNPs in the pores of mesoporous carbon have also been reported [11 -13]. Furthermore, MNPs have been used for immunochemical staining, pollutant removal, and the promotion of stem cell growth by replacing the role of conventional horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [14 -16]. Although the above-mentioned examples demonstrate the potential of MNPs to replace natural peroxidase, numerous limitations should still be overcome for their widespread utilization, such as low specificity and activity, monotonous catalysis based on a redox-type reaction, potential toxicity, and decreased activity after surface modifications. Among them, surface modifications of MNPs should be performed for the applications in biosensors and immunoassays to obtain the required functionalities. For example, Yan ' s group modified MNPs with SiO2, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), dextran, or PEG as chemical linkers to conjugate antibody molecules; however, the surface coating shields the available surface and catalytic site from the substrate, consequently resulting in lowered catalytic activity [2]. Thus, there is a significant incentive to develop effectively modified MNPs that exhibit higher peroxidase activity with abundant chemical functionalities. Along this line, we present a colorimetric biosensor for the determination of H2O2and glucose by using the peroxidase activity of poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) derivativefunctionalized Fe3O4magnetic nanoparticles (PEG-MNPs). Although PEGylation on MNPs with free PEG molecules has been reported to provide solubility in aqueous buffer solution [1, 2], PEG-derivative modified MNPs have not been widely investigated as peroxidase mimetics. Phosphorousbased ligands such as phosphine oxide or phospholipid were additionally derivatized with PEG to provide further steric stabilization of MNPs and active functional groups that can be used to conjugate biological receptors [17]. The PEGMNPs yielded a significant enhancement on the catalytic activity of MNPs by preventing self-aggregation and facilitating transfer of H2O2and ABTS substrate toward MNPs via hydrophilic interaction. The catalytic performance of the newly synthesized PEG-MNPs and their analytical application for visual detection of glucose were systematically demonstrated (e.g., specificity, linearity, and sensitivity). # Materials and methods # Materials 2,2 ′ -Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzo-thiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS), 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), β -D-glucose, arabinose, fructose, lactose, Glucose oxidase (GOx) from Aspergillus niger , oleic acid (90%), poly(ethylene glycol) methyl ether (Mn: 2000), phosphorus oxychloride (99%), APTES, 1-octadecene (90%), human serum, and glucose assay kit were all purchased from SigmaAldrich (Milwaukee, WI, http://www.sigmaaldrich.com), and35% H2O2was purchased from Junsei Chemical Co. (Japan, http://www.junsei.co.jp). Iron(III) chloride hexa-hydrate (97 %) was purchased from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, http://www.alfa.co.kr). Sodium oleate (97%) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Japan, http://www. t c i c h e m i c a l s . c o m ) . 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethylene glycol)2000] (mPEG-2000PE) was purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids (Alabaster, AL, https://avantilipids.com). Chloroform (99.5%), tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.5%), n-hexane (95%), and acetone were purchased from Samchun chemicals (South Korea, http://www.samchun.com). All other chemicals were of analytical grade or higher. # Synthesis of water-dispersible PEG-MNPs, bare MNPs, and APTES-modified MNPs Water-dispersible MNPs were prepared by two different water-dispersing methods, ligand exchange with poly(ethylene glycol)-derivatized phosphine oxide (POPEG) ligands and encapsulation by PEG-phospholipid shell (Lipid PEG) [17, 18]. First, oleic acid-stabilized MNPs were synthesized using thermal decomposition of Fe-oleate in high-boilingpoint solvents using procedures developed by Hyeon and co-workers [19]. Two different approaches were applied to disperse oleic acid-stabilized MNPs in aqueous media. The first is cap exchange by POPEG, which has a PEG segment and a surfacecoordinating phosphine oxide head group, for displacing the oleic acid on the nanoparticles. We denoted the MNPs as POPEG-MNPs. Briefly, 10mg of nanoparticles were dispersed in3mL of THF solution containing50mg of POPEG. After removing THF by evacuation, the sticky mixture was incubated at80 °C under a vacuum for3h. DI water was added, and the dispersed nanoparticles were purified by syringe and centrifugal filters. The other approach was the preparation by encapsulation via a hydrophobic interaction between alkyl chains in Lipid PEG and oleic acid on the nanoparticles. We also denoted the MNPs as Lipid PEG-MNPs, where10mg of oleic acid-stabilized MNPs were dispersed in2mL of chloroform. Then, it was mixed with1mL of chloroform solution containing10mg of mPEG-2000PE. After evaporating the solvent, it was incubated at80 °C in a vacuum for1h. After the addition of DI water, Lipid PEGstabilized MNPs were purified by filtration and ultra-speed centrifugation. After synthesis, the resulting POPEG-MNPs and Lipid PEG-MNPs were re-dispersed in water to create a stock solution with a total Fe concentration of2.67mg·mL -1and1.91mg·mL -1 , respectively, and stored at4 °C for use. Bare MNPs with the hydroxyl group were synthesized using the reported procedure [20]. Briefly, a1M sodium hydroxide solution was added to a0.25M Fe3+ and Fe2+ aqueous salt solutions (2:1M ratio) while stirring at80 °C for40min (pH10). The MNPs obtained in this manner were washed several times with water and ethanol and dried at70 °C under a vacuum. MNPs modified with APTES (APTES-MNPs) were synthesized using the procedures reported by Gao et al. [21]. The MNPs were dispersed in water to make a10mg·mL -1stock solution and stored at4 °C until use. The synthesized MNPs were analzyed by transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images on a Jeol EM-2010microscope (Jeol Co.). The hydrodynamic diameters (HDs) of MNPs were determined by light-scattering analysis using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Co.). Iron concentrations in MNPs were also measured by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) (model: Polyscan60E, Thermo Jarrell). # H2O2detection using PEG-MNPs (POPEG- & Lipid PEG-MNPs), bare MNPs, and APTES-MNPs To investigate the peroxidase-like activity of PEG-MNPs, the catalytic oxidation of ABTS in the presence of pre-determined concentrations of H2O2was performed in a96-well plate as follows: A solution containing140 μ Lof sodium acetate buffer (0.2M, pH4.0), 20 μ Lof1mg·mL -1PEG-MNPs,20 μ Lof100mM H2O2, and20 μ L of60mM ABTS was added into a well and incubated at room temperature (RT) for5min. After the reaction, the MNPs were separated from the mixtures in each well by the application of a strong external magnetic field for30s. The supernatant was subsequently transferred to a well of new96-well plate and used to capture color images. Spectrophotometric measurements were conducted in a scanning mode or at417nm using a microplate reader (Synergy H1, BioTek, VT). The effects of reaction buffer pH on the activity of PEGMNPs were investigated following the same procedures but using0.2M acetate buffer solutions prepared from pH2to10. The effects of incubation temperature on the activity of POPEG-MNPs and Lipid PEG-MNPs were also investigated following the same procedures but incubated in different temperatures from4to60 °C. The relative activity (%) was calculated by using the ratio of the residual activity to the original activity before the incubation of each sample. # Kinetic analysis Steady-state kinetic assays were carried out at RT in a1.5mL cuvette. For the kinetic assay of TMB, a solution was prepared containing0.02mg·mL -1of POPEG-MNPs or Lipid PEGMNPs and1M H2O2in1mL of sodium acetate buffer (0.2M, pH4.0), and TMB concentration varied. For the kinetic assay of H2O2, TMB (final800 μ M) was added in1mL of reaction buffer with varied concentrations of H2O2. After the substrates were mixed, color changes were observed immediately. All reactions were monitored using a kinetic mode at652nm. The kinetic parameters were calculated based on the equation ν = Vmax x [S]/( Km + [S]), where ν is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal velocity, [S] is the concentration of substrate, and Km is the Michaelis constant. The catalytic constant kcat was derived from kcat = Vmax /[E], where [E] is the iron concentration measured by ICP-AES. # Determination of glucose using PEG-MNPs (POPEG- & Lipid PEG-MNPs) and GOx The detection of glucose concentration was performed in a96well plate as follows: 24 μ L of10mg·mL -1GOx and120 μ L of glucose at different concentrations (1 μ M -5mM)were first incubated in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (10mM, pH7.0) at37 °C for20min. Then, 24 μ L of60mM ABTS, 24 μ L of10mg·mL -1PEG-MNPs, and48 μ L of sodium acetate buffer (0.5M, pH4.0) were additionally added into each well and incubated at RT for10min. After the reaction, the PEG-MNPs were separated and the supernatant was further analyzed by measuring the absorbance (417nm), as described above. Other carbohydrates such as arabinose, fructose, and lactose were used instead of glucose for selectivity test. # Glucose detection in human serum Initial glucose concentration of human serum was first determined by glucose assay kit (Sigma-Aldrich) and then predetermined amount of glucose was further added into human serum to make spiked samples, which represent normal, boundary, and high level of blood glucose. Finally, glucose concentration of each spiked sample (120 μ L, 10-fold dilution) was determined by the same procedures decribed above. # Results and discussion # Construction of PEG-MNPs for colorimetric quantification of H2O2and glucose As most of the peroxidase activity of MNPs stems from surface atoms, a large surface area available for the catalytic event is necessary for obtaining high catalytic activity. Thus, we designed MNPs having PEG-derivatives that can be monodispersed in aqueous buffer without self-aggregation, thereby providing highly enhanced peroxidase activity. The highly active MNPs can also be applied to detect glucose by coupling with GOx catalysis (Fig. 1). To this goal, we prepared two different PEG-derivatized ligands, POPEG and Lipid PEG, and modified the surface of Fig. 1Schematic illustration of the enhanced peroxidase activity of PEG derivative-modified MNPs compared to that of bare MNPs for the colorimetric detection of glucose by coupling with GOx catalysis MNPs with each ligand. POPEG and Lipid PEG modified MNPs were synthesized via extremely simple strategies of cap exchange and encapsulation by hydrophobic interaction, respectively, compared to the conventional surface modification methods generally achieved by passivating the MNPs with silica or polymer shells [22]. TEM analysis clearly reveals the monodispersed state of PEG-MNPs, whereas the conventionally synthesized MNPs are prone to aggregation (Fig. 2). In order to study the stability of aqueous dispersion of MNPs, the hydrodynamic diameters (HDs) of MNPs were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. S1). The average HDs of POPEG-MNPs and Lipid PEG-MNPs were29.6nm and34.7nm, respectively, where the increase of size was resulted from PEGylated shells by POPEG and Lipid PEG. On the other hand, bare MNPs synthesized by coprecipitation had much larger average HD (72.7nm) than both PEG-MNPs. In particular, bare MNPs had two distributions of HD resulted from interparticular aggregations, demonstrating that bare MNPs were not stable in aqueous media. # Investigation for peroxidase-like activity of PEG-MNPs The peroxidase activities of PEG-MNPs, bare MNPs, and APTES-MNPs were examined by performing the representative peroxidase-catalyzed ABTS oxidation reactions in the presence of H2O2, and the responses were monitored using absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 3). The results of this experiment show that the PEG-MNPs have highly enhanced ABTS oxidation rates and, consequently, much more intense colorimetric responses take place in contrast to those systems containing bare MNPs or APTES-MNPs. There is no obvious difference in colorimetric responses between POPEG-MNPs and Lipid PEG-MNPs, confirming their similar peroxidase activity to oxidize ABTS. Observations made in study on the effects of pH and temperature on the catalytic activity of POPEG-MNPs and Lipid PEG-MNPs, showed that pH4.0and45 °C were ideal assay conditions (Fig. S2and S3). Although the incubation at45 °C resulted in maximal peroxidase activity, the incubation at RT was carried out rather than45 °C because of the practical convenience and sufficiently Fig. 2TEMimagesofbare MNPsprepared by the co-precipitation method a , POPEG ligand-exchanged MNPs b , and Lipid PEG-encapsulated MNPs c acceptable catalytic performance of MNPs at RT (about75% of maximum activity). PEG-MNPs were also proved to be stable over a broad range of pH from2to9and temperature from4to90 °C, whereas HRP lost most of its activity at an extreme pH or temperature conditions (Fig. S4), demonstrating their practical applicability. To elucidate further the peroxidase activity of both POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNPs, steady-state kinetic constants were determined and compared with those of bare MNPs (Fig. S5, Table S1). Both the apparent Km values of the POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNPs for TMB were about7 -8% higher than that of bare MNPs, indicating the mass transfer limit after the modification of PEG-ligands is not significantly increased. Moreover, the Km values of the POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNPs for H2O2were even significantly lower than that of bare MNPs and determined to be only ~20% of the value for bare MNPs. This indicates that the modification of PEGderivatized ligands on the surface of MNPs resulted in a significantly enhanced affinity toward H2O2, possibly due to the excellent dispersion of both POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNPs in aqueous buffer and hydrophilic PEG segments of the ligands that facilitate substrate transfer [4]. Furthermore, kcat values of POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNPs for both TMB and H2O2were found to be generally higher up to2-fold, indicating that PEG-MNPs leads to an enhanced activity compared with that of bare MNPs, which are prone to aggregation. Overall, both POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNPs have an approximately6-fold higher catalytic efficiency ( kcat/Km ) for H2O2compared to that of bare MNPs. # Analytical capabilities of PEG-MNPs for the detection of glucose The feasibility of the method was demonstrated by coupling GOx with either POPEG- or Lipid PEG-MNPs. In the presence of target glucose, GOx catalyzed the oxidation of glucose to generate H2O2, which should activate PEG-MNPs to convert selected substrates into colored product. Through the typical colorimetric reaction, glucose was successfully detected by the generation of a visibly or spectroscopically detected color change (Figs. 4). On the other hand, in either case, no colorimetric signal or absorbance peak was observed in the negative control samples, where other carbohydrates (arabinose, fructose, and lactose) similar to the target glucose are used even at10-fold higher concentrations. This result clearly demonstrates that the

Table2Detection precision of the POPEG-MNP-based biosensor for the determination of glucose levels in spiked human blood samples a Mean of six independent measurements b Standard deviation of six measurements c Coefficient of variation d Measured value / Expected value × 100 present PEG-MNP-based biosensor shows excellent specificity to the corresponding glucose. From an analysis of doseresponse curves using both POPEG- and Lipid PEG-MNP systems, the limit of detection (LOD) for glucose was determined to be as low as3 μ M in the linear range from5to1000 μ M (Fig. 5). Although the previous reports using GOx/HRP bienzyme system showed a lower detection limit down to0.3 μ M, the LOD and linear range values of our system are among the best results describing the various peroxidase mimics-based colorimetric detection of glucose (Table1) [2, 23 -32]. Considering the excellent selectivity and sensitivity along with the merits of MNPs as peroxidase mimetics, it is evident that our method can serve as a promising alternative to existing methods for the detection of glucose. # Diagnosis of hyperglycemia using clinical blood samples Finally, we evaluated the diagnostic capability of the selected PEG-MNP system by using real human blood samples that contained representative levels of glucose corresponding to the normal, boundary, and high stage of hyperglycemia (normal; ≤ 5.6mM, boundary; 5.6 ~ 7mM, and high; >7mM) [33]. Original amount of glucose in serum sample was first determined by glucose assay kit (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and then predetermined amount of glucose was further added to make the representative levels. As a result, the serum glucose levels were quantified with excellent precisions yielding CVs in a range of4.3 -7% and recovery rates of97.9 -103.8% (Table2), verifying the excellent reproducibility and reliability of the method. These results show that the POPEG-MNPbased assay system should serve as a promising analytical platform to diagnose high level of glucose in clinical settings. # Conclusion We herein developed an efficient colorimetric biosensor for detecting and determining the concentrations of glucose based on the enhanced peroxidase activity of POPEG- or Lipid PEG-modified MNPs. The results of the investigations demonstrate that the PEG-MNP-based system incorporating GOx catalysis has high selectivities and sensitivities for the detection of the corresponding glucose. As the current system enabled the rapid visual detection of target molecules with the peroxidase mimicking activity of PEG-MNPs, it should find practical applications in facility-limited or point-of-care testing (POCT) environments without the addition of natural peroxidase enzyme. Because the MNPs can rival natural peroxidase enzymes due to their high stability, robustness, and low cost by facile scale-up, the analytical platform based on effectively modified MNPs can be further extended to many potential applications in biosensors in the future. Acknowledgements This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (NRF2014R1A1A1003555and NRF-2014R1A1A1006016) and by the Gachon University Gil Medical Center (Grant number: 2014-22). Curr Opin Cardiol29(4):381 -388 # Supplementary Information # Visual determination of hydrogen peroxide and glucose by exploiting the peroxidase-like activity of magnetic nanoparticles functionalized with a poly(ethylene glycol) derivative Ho Yun Shin, 1Bong-Geun Kim, 2Seongyeon Cho, 1Jongwon Lee, 2Hyon Bin Na2 * and Moon Il Kim1 * 1Department of BioNano Technology, Gachon University, 1342Seongnamdae-ro, Sujeonggu, Seongnam, Gyeonggi13120, Republic of Korea. 2Department of Chemical Engineering, Myongji University, 116Myongji-ro, Cheoin-gu, Yongin, Gyeonggi17058, Republic of Korea *To whom correspondence should be addressed. POPEG-MNPs and HRP were first incubated at (a) pH2-9or (b) 4-90 ºC for2h and then their peroxidase activities were measured at a standard reaction condition (pH4.0and RT). The maximum relative activity in the y-axis of each curve was set as100%. Figure S5. Steady-state kinetic assays of POPEG-MNPs and Lipid PEG-MNPs for TMB and H2O2 ([a] and [b]), and their corresponding double reciprocal (Lineweaver-Burk) plots of activity ([c] and [d]). Error bars represent standard errors derived from three independent measurements, and the y-axis values ( V : initial velocity) are obtained from the observed absorbance values at652nm.
